title: Phases of a Compiler
subject: System Programming & Compiler Construction (SPCC)
syllabus_reference: Module 3: Introduction to Compilers (Phases of Compiler)
textbook_reference: Alfred V. Aho & Monica S. Lam, Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools, Chapter 1, Page 4

recent_questions: Explain different phases of a compiler with a neat schematic diagram (May 2024).
Describe the role of Lexical Analyzer and Syntax Analyzer with an example (Dec 2023).

theory: A compiler is a program translator that converts source code written in a high-level programming language into target machine code. This process is divided into multiple sequential phases. (1) Lexical Analyzer (Lexer): Converts source characters into a stream of tokens. (2) Syntax Analyzer (Parser): Group tokens into grammatical structures (Parse Tree/Syntax Tree). (3) Semantic Analyzer: Checks for type compatibility and semantic constraints. (4) Intermediate Code Generator: Produces machine-independent low-level code (e.g. three-address code). (5) Code Optimizer: Improves intermediate code speed and resource utilization. (6) Code Generator: Translates optimized code into target assembly or machine language. Throughout compilation, the Symbol Table tracks variable declarations, and the Error Handler reports syntax or type violations.
key_points: Lexical Analysis (Scanner): Groups characters into tokens using Regular Expressions.
Syntax Analysis (Parser): Establishes syntactic structure using Context-Free Grammar.
Semantic Analysis: Checks types, scope, and semantic rules (e.g., undeclared variables).
Intermediate Code (ICG): Uses representation like Quadruples or Triples.
Code Optimization: Minimizes execution time and space (e.g., loop unrolling, dead code removal).
use_cases: Building compilers, interpreters, and static analysis tools.
Building syntax highlighters and code refactoring plugins.

phases.label: Lexical Analyzer
phases.desc: Scans character input stream, generates token stream

phases.label: Syntax Analyzer
phases.desc: Constructs syntax tree checking grammar rules

phases.label: Semantic Analyzer
phases.desc: Performs type checking and semantic validation

phases.label: Code Optimizer
phases.desc: Transforms code to improve speed and memory efficiency

phases.label: Target Code Generator
phases.desc: Produces final machine code or assembly language

title: Parse Tree for id + id * id
subject: System Programming & Compiler Construction (SPCC)
textbook_reference: Alfred V. Aho, Monica S. Lam, Ravi Sethi & Jeffrey D. Ullman, Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools (Dragon Book), 2nd Edition, Chapter 2, Section 2.2

recent_questions: Construct a parse tree for the expression id + id * id using suitable grammar (Dec 2023).
Define parse tree. Draw parse tree and syntax tree for string id + id * id (May 2024).

theory: A parse tree (or concrete syntax tree) is a graphical representation of the hierarchical syntactic structure of a sentence derived from a context-free grammar (CFG). During the syntax analysis phase of a compiler, the parser processes the token stream generated by the lexical analyzer and structures it hierarchically according to the grammar rules. In the standard arithmetic grammar (E -> E + T | T, T -> T * F | F, F -> id), the parse tree for the expression 'id + id * id' structures the multiplication '*' deeper in the tree under 'T', thereby naturally enforcing the mathematical precedence of multiplication over addition.
key_points: A parse tree shows the concrete syntactic derivation of a string from the grammar's start symbol.
The root of the tree is the start symbol (E), and leaf nodes (terminals) read left-to-right yield the parsed expression.
Operator precedence is represented by nesting higher-precedence operators (*) lower (deeper) in the tree structure.
Useful for verifying grammatical correctness and forms the basis for constructing the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
use_cases: Syntax validation in parsers for compilers and interpreters.
Translation schemas for syntax-directed translation (SDT) and semantic analysis.
Expression evaluation in domain-specific languages and calculations engines.

title: Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) — Strings Ending in '01'
subject: Theory of Computation (TOC)
textbook_reference: John E. Hopcroft, Jeffrey D. Ullman, Rajeev Motwani, Introduction to Automata Theory, Languages and Computation, Pearson, Chapter 2, Page 48

recent_questions: Design a DFA to accept all strings over {0,1} ending with '01'. Show transition table and diagram (Dec 2023).
Explain DFA with a suitable example. Define the 5-tuple for the DFA that accepts strings ending in '01' (May 2024).

theory: A DFA is a 5-tuple (Q, Σ, δ, q0, F) where δ is a total function. For strings over {0,1} ending in '01': Q={q0,q1,q2}, Σ={0,1}, start state q0, F={q2}. State q0: last symbol was '1' or start; q1: last symbol was '0'; q2: last two symbols were '01' (accepting).
key_points: DFA 5-tuple: (Q, Σ, δ, q0, F) — δ is total (defined for every state-symbol pair).
q2 is the only accepting state — reached when last two symbols were '01'.
DFAs are equivalent in power to NFAs (Rabin-Scott theorem) and regular expressions.
Language L = {w ∈ {0,1}* | w ends in '01'} is a regular language.
use_cases: Pattern matching and regular expression evaluation.
Network protocol state machines and traffic filtering.
